course_description:
The aim of this course is to develop students’ foundational knowledge and skills related to professional nursing practice and the care of individuals with chronic health issues. The course is grounded in the principles of person-centred care and considers the unique values, needs and preferences of the individual and their support systems, including families, and the registered nurse's role in providing holistic, person-centred care. Students will develop theoretical knowledge and practical skills in health promotion, prevention, and early intervention in the context of chronic health conditions. They will learn to apply the clinical reasoning cycle to assess, plan, implement and evaluate care. Throughout the course, students will explore the key importance of interprofessional collaboration and approaches that promote self-management to improve health outcomes and quality of life for individuals with chronic health conditions and their families.

The course content will be delivered over a 10 week period via a lecture series, in-person inquiry-based learning, and self-directed online learning activities.
timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: The aim of this course is to provide students with opportunities to explore the nature and lived experience of individuals who have chronic health conditions. A key tenet of this course is the centrality of the individual and their support systems, including families, and the registered nurse’s role in supporting these people. The course is situated in a primary healthcare framework and enables students to develop their theoretical knowledge and skills around health promotion, prevention, early intervention and the promotion of self-management in the context of chronic conditions. In addition, students will explore care planning, interprofessional teamwork, rehabilitative care and palliative care. The course content will be delivered utilising inquiry based learning, lectures, online learning activities and self-directed learning.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and evaluate common chronic health conditions and their management in a variety of care settings.
Apply and evaluate a population health approach to understanding chronic conditions to facilitate health promotion and prevention principles when assessing individuals.
Critically analyse and apply evidence-based approaches to supporting individuals with a chronic health issue and their significant others.

Critically analyse and evaluate differences between models of care in acute and non-acute healthcare environments to explore and evaluate the role of person-centred care in the context of chronic illness.
Critically analyse the role of the registered nurse and the interprofessional team in supporting individuals with chronic conditions.

assessment_details.title: Online Quiz (In-Class)
assessment_details.due_date: 24/03/2026 8:00 am This online invigilated MCQ quiz will be completed in-class. Please note that you will only be able to access and complete the quiz at this time. Check your timetable to comfirm the day, time and venue. You will need to arrive at the entry to the timetabled room 10 minutes before the scheduled start time.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The MCQ quiz will be accessible through the course Blackboard site. Questions will cover content from the Introduction Module and Module 1 (Topics 1-4), as well as the associated lectures and tutorials from Weeks 1-4. You will have 60 minutes to answer 40 multiple choice questions. Each question is worth 0.5 marks, totalling 20 marks. Students will only have ONE ATTEMPT to complete this quiz. Please refer to the assessment task sheet, which can be accessed via the course Blackboard site. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The quiz will be accessible through the course Blackboard site and will self-submit after 60 minutes. This is an identity verified assessment, and instructors will check your student ID as you enter the room. Students who arrive late or who do not have their student ID card with them will not be permitted to enter the room and will fail this assessment with zero (0) marks. Students who are not in class and identity verified when they sit this online MCQ quiz will fail this assessment with zero (0) marks.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
In the case of extenuating circumstances where a student cannot sit for this MCQ test on the due date, please make sure to apply for an in-semester deferred examination. Please do not apply for an end of semester deferred examination. You will need to supply documentary evidence such as a medical certificate.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
The online quiz will lock and cannot be commenced once the due date and time have passed. The quiz will save and submit automatically once the due date and time have passed.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam .
assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Oral Viva and Group Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 2/04/2026 1:00 pm Part A (Oral Viva) presubmission document must be submitted by 1pm on Thursday of Week 6 (2.4.26) prior to oral vivas in Week 7. Part B (Group Presentation) PowerPoint files must be submitted by 1pm on Friday of Week 8 (24.4.26) prior to group presentations which will be delivered and assessed during timetabled Week 9 IBL sessions.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Peer assessment factor, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment is completed in two parts : Part A (Oral Viva), followed by Part B (Group Presentation). Together, the two parts of this assessment task examine your ability to locate, evaluate, communicate, and apply high-quality scholarly evidence to the development of a community-based chronic illness management program. Part A is an individual assessment designed to support your preparation for the group oral presentation (Part B). In Part A (Oral Viva), you will demonstrate your ability to locate and evaluate high-quality evidence appropriate for use in developing a community-based chronic condition management program and explain how this evidence can be used to inform nursing practice. In Part B, you will work in small groups to develop and deliver a PowerPoint presentation that demonstrates your understanding of the needs of the person with chronic illness as they live in the community, and the role of the registered nurse as a coordinator of care. Students will work as a team to develop a program for people with chronic illness that builds their self-efficacy and ability to self-manage their illness. The task description and marking rubric for this assessment are available via the "Assessments" tab on the NURS7126 Blackboard site. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Part A: Upload a copy of your presubmission document (Sources of Evidence table) via the submission point on Blackboard by the due date (prior to your viva).
Part B: One member of each group will upload a copy of their group’s PowerPoint presentation file via the submission point on Blackboard by the due date (prior to your group presentation). Please check the companion website assessment area for specific instructions on this process. A link will be made available in the assessment area of your Blackboard course to allow you to submit your presentation electronically.
Please refer to ITS help if you experience difficulties in submitting your work via Blackboard as instructed on the companion website.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Applying for an extension
Extension requests are submitted online via my.UQ. Extension requests received in any other way will not be approved.
Extension guidelines
· Extension requests should be submitted prior to the assessment deadline.
· Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date may not be approved. Late requests must include evidence of the reasons for the late request as per the guidelines in my.UQ .
· The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of illness or disruption caused by the exceptional circumstances and will typically be no more than the specified maximum extension length for each assessment.
· A maximum of three assessment requests can be submitted for each assessment, and the third request must include an Assessment Management Plan.
· Students who have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 4 weeks in a semester are advised to carefully consider whether they are capable of successfully completing their courses and may need to consider applying for removal of course .
· Students who have a disability or a chronic medical condition (including a mental health condition) are encouraged to meet with a Disability Advisor within Student Services to request a Student Access Plan (SAP) with a clause on extension requests. Students who have an SAP can apply for a 7-day extension.
Outcome of application
Extension requests are generally processed within 2 working days.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Part A: Late submission of the pre-submission document (without an approved extension) will incur a penalty of 10% of the marks available for Part A per day late. Work submitted more than seven (7) days after the due date (without an approved extension) will not receive a mark. If you miss your oral viva timeslot through lack of planning and/or poor organisation, you will receive a mark of 0/100 for Part A. If you miss your oral viva timeslot through some other error, depending on circumstance, limited “make-up” slots are available in Week 8 but will incur a 10% late penalty. If there are extenuating circumstances, evidence of these will need to be submitted as an extension request and, if approved, your session will be rescheduled in Week 8. Rescheduled vivas are managed the same as deferred assessment – there is no option for the viva to be rescheduled a second time.
Part B: Late submission of the PowerPoint presentation file (without an approved extension) will incur a penalty of 10% of the available marks per day late. Work submitted more than seven (7) days after the due date (without an approved extension) will not receive a mark.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 10 (23 Feb - 10 May)
learning_activities.topic: Online Learning Modules You are expected to spend approximately five (5) hours per week on online learning for this course. This includes working through the online modules and recommended and required readings. Your online modules are housed in UQ Extend, and can be accessed via the Course Resources section of the Blackboard site for this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Recorded lectures and any accompanying resources will be made available via the Course Resources section on the companion website for this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
